email.subject: FSA contribution question
email.body: Hi, I'm setting up my elections for next year and I thought the max I could put into my Health FSA was $3,200. A coworker mentioned it went up. Can you confirm what the actual limit is for 2026, and whether there's a minimum I have to contribute too?

expect.notes: 2026 Health FSA max = $3,300 (up from $3,200); minimum = $120. Straightforward lookup.

result.question:
Subject: FSA contribution question

Hi, I'm setting up my elections for next year and I thought the max I could put into my Health FSA was $3,200. A coworker mentioned it went up. Can you confirm what the actual limit is for 2026, and whether there's a minimum I have to contribute too?
result.answer:
Hi there,

You're correct that the limit went up! For 2026, the maximum you can contribute to your Health FSA is $3,300—an increase from the $3,200 limit you mentioned. There is no required minimum contribution, but if you do contribute, the minimum amount is $120.

Best regards,
Benefits Team

citations.quote: Effective January 1, 2026 the maximum amount you're allowed to contribute to the Chevron Health FSA is increasing: • You can contribute up to $3,300 (increase from $3,200).* • The minimum contribution remains $120.

email.subject: Orthodontist coverage for my daughter
email.body: My daughter needs braces and her orthodontist isn't in our dental network. What's the lifetime maximum the plan will pay for orthodontic care if we go out-of-network vs. staying in-network? Trying to figure out how much we'll be on the hook for.

expect.notes: Conflict trap: current (2022) ortho lifetime max = $2,000 network / $1,000 out-of-network. Older 2017 sections say $1,500 network. Must use the most recent via effective_date.

result.question:
Subject: Orthodontist coverage for my daughter

My daughter needs braces and her orthodontist isn't in our dental network. What's the lifetime maximum the plan will pay for orthodontic care if we go out-of-network vs. staying in-network? Trying to figure out how much we'll be on the hook for.
result.answer:
Hi there,

Your plan's orthodontic lifetime maximums depend on which providers you choose. Here's the breakdown under the current plan (effective January 1, 2022):

**Network (in-network orthodontist):** The plan will pay up to $2,000 in lifetime benefits, covering 50 percent of contracted fees.

**Out-of-network:** The plan will pay up to $1,000 in lifetime benefits, covering 50 percent of Delta Dental's allowance.

One important thing to note: The two pools are combined. Any benefits you receive from out-of-network care count toward your out-of-network maximum, but they also count toward your total network maximum. For example, if your daughter receives $500 in out-of-network orthodontic benefits, she could still qualify for up to $1,500 more from the network coverage (not the full $2,000), bringing the total plan payment to $2,000.

Since your daughter's orthodontist is out-of-network, it's a good idea to ask the orthodontist to request a predetermination of benefits from Delta Dental before treatment starts. That way you'll know exactly what your out-of-pocket costs will be.

Best regards,
Benefits Team

citations.quote: Effective January 1, 2022 the orthodontic care lifetime maximum is as follows: • Network coverage: There is a lifetime benefit maximum of $2,000 per plan member. • Out-of-network coverage: There is a lifetime benefit maximum of $1,000 per plan member.

Network coverage: Pays 50 percent of the contracted fees for braces and other teeth-straightening services and appliances... There is a lifetime benefit maximum of $1,500 per plan member. • Out-of-network coverage: Pays 50 percent of Delta Dentals allowance for braces and other teeth-straightening services and appliances... There is a lifetime benefit maximum of $1,000 per plan member.

Any network orthodontia benefits you use count toward your maximum out-of-network orthodontia benefits. Similarly, any out-of-network orthodontia benefits you use count toward your maximum network orthodontia benefits.

email.subject: Travel costs for a specialist out of state
email.body: My doctor is referring me to a specialist for a procedure that isn't available anywhere near me. Does our medical plan cover any of the travel or hotel costs to go get this done out of state? Not sure if this is something the plan even considers.

expect.notes: Answerable: Medical PPO has a travel-expense reimbursement provision (not reasonably available within 100 miles, service must be covered, caregiver may be included).

result.question:
Subject: Travel costs for a specialist out of state

My doctor is referring me to a specialist for a procedure that isn't available anywhere near me. Does our medical plan cover any of the travel or hotel costs to go get this done out of state? Not sure if this is something the plan even considers.
result.answer:
Hi there,

Good news – yes, your Chevron Medical PPO Plan does cover travel and lodging costs for out-of-state specialist care. "This plan provision reimburses you for charges incurred for reasonable, qualifying travel expenses that are essential to receive any covered medical service under the Chevron Medical PPO Plan."

Your situation appears to qualify. The benefit applies when "the covered service is not reasonably available to you from a network provider or facility within 100 miles of your home residence" – which matches your scenario if the procedure truly isn't available nearby.

Qualifying reimbursable expenses include:
- Mileage for your personal car
- Rental cars
- Airline or train tickets
- Hotel lodging (subject to IRS per diem limits)
- Parking fees, tolls, and ride-share services

Important points: The travel benefit does not count toward your deductible, and the specialist's procedure itself will continue to be covered under the plan's normal medical benefits. Meals and personal items are not covered. You'll need to file a travel claim with documentation no later than six months after the year in which the service is provided.

I'd recommend contacting the plan administrator before your trip to confirm the procedure is a covered medical service and to understand any specific requirements or preauthorization needs.

Best regards,
Benefits Team

citations.quote: This plan provision reimburses you for charges incurred for reasonable, qualifying travel expenses that are essential to receive any covered medical service under the Chevron Medical PPO Plan.
The covered service is not reasonably available to you from a network provider or facility within 100 miles of your home residence.

General examples of qualifying travel expenses include but are not limited to: Mileage in your personal car to/from your home to the covered service provider or facility, Lodging not provided by a hospital or other institution for the patient and caregiver, subject to IRS per diem limits, Rental cars, Train or airline travel tickets, Bus, shuttle, taxi and ride share services, Long-term airport parking or other parking fees

Reimbursement is not subject to the annual deductible. While benefits for the covered medical service will continue to follow the Medical PPO Plan's normal deductible, coinsurance and copayment schedule, there is no separate deductible, coinsurance or copayment requirement for reimbursement of qualifying travel expenses.

email.subject: Did my critical illness coverage change?
email.body: I got a notice that said something about a new administrator for my hospital indemnity and critical illness coverage. Do I need to do anything to keep my current coverage, or does it just carry over automatically? Wasn't sure if this required action on my end.

expect.notes: Spans two docs. Judgment case: if the docs state coverage carries over with no action, answer that; if they don't state an action requirement, flag the uncertainty rather than guessing.

result.question:
Subject: Did my critical illness coverage change?

I got a notice that said something about a new administrator for my hospital indemnity and critical illness coverage. Do I need to do anything to keep my current coverage, or does it just carry over automatically? Wasn't sure if this required action on my end.
result.answer:
Hi there,

Good news—your coverage will carry over automatically. Since you are currently enrolled, your Critical Illness and Hospital Indemnity coverage will automatically continue in 2026 with the new administrator, Securian, unless you decide to make changes. Your coverage has already been automatically assigned to the closest available option with Securian.

While no action is required if you're satisfied with your coverage, we do encourage you to verify your coverage details on BenefitConnect during open enrollment (October 20–31, 2025) and make any additional adjustments if needed. Any changes you make during that window will take effect January 1, 2026.

Best regards,
Benefits Team

citations.section: what happens to your current coverage
citations.quote: If you are currently enrolled, your Critical Illness and/or Hospital Indemnity election will automatically continue in 2026 unless you decide to make changes during open enrollment, October 20 through October 31, 2025.

email.subject: Enrolling in long-term care insurance
email.body: I've been meaning to sign up for the long-term care insurance plan for a couple years and never got around to it. I want to enroll now — can you send me the enrollment steps? Want to make sure I'm getting the same plan my manager has, since she signed up a while back.

expect.notes: Two-part: enrollment steps may be answerable from LongTermCare.pdf, BUT 'the same plan my manager has' is an unknowable personal fact -> must flag that part and not guess. Watch enrollment eligibility windows too.

result.question:
Subject: Enrolling in long-term care insurance

I've been meaning to sign up for the long-term care insurance plan for a couple years and never got around to it. I want to enroll now — can you send me the enrollment steps? Want to make sure I'm getting the same plan my manager has, since she signed up a while back.
result.answer:
Hi there,

I'm glad you're ready to enroll in the Long-Term Care Insurance Plan. Here are the enrollment steps:

**Enrollment options:**
- Apply online at **genworth.com/chevron** (available anytime)
- Call **Genworth at 1-800-416-3624**
- Visit **hr2.chevron.com/OpenEnrollment** for additional resources

[Source 4] notes that "you must submit an enrollment application" and provides these resources to "apply for this coverage, learn more about the program, and get a rate quote." Once approved, [Source 4] states that "you will receive a Certificate of Coverage from Genworth within 30 days after you are approved for enrollment in the New LTC plan."

**Important note:** The sources don't specify what plan your manager enrolled in or whether the same plan options are still available now. I'd recommend contacting Genworth or the HR Service Center (1-888-825-5247) to confirm your eligibility and review current plan options before enrolling.

Best regards,
Benefits Team

citations.quote: you must submit an enrollment application. You can apply for this coverage at any time on or after October 14, 2019. To learn more about the program, get a rate quote and enroll, use the following resources: • Apply anytime on or after October 26, 2019: genworth.com/chevron • Call Genworth at 1-800-416-3624 starting October 14, 2019.
You will receive a Certificate of Coverage from Genworth within 30 days after you are approved for enrollment in the New LTC plan.
